original_hypothesis.title: Isolate Liquidation Fails to Clear Locker Address, Permanently Bricking Re-Deposited NFTs
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: When an isolate loan is liquidated, the protocol fails to reset the NFT's `lockerAddr`. If the liquidated NFT is ever re-deposited into the protocol, it becomes permanently stuck and cannot be withdrawn.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: `executeIsolateLiquidate` deletes `loanData` and clears the owner, but does not reset `tokenData.lockerAddr` to `address(0)`. If the NFT is re-deposited, `validateWithdrawERC721` will revert because it strictly requires `lockerAddr == address(0)` for all withdrawals.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Clears owner and supplyMode but omits tokenData.lockerAddr = address(0)

preconditions: An NFT is liquidated via isolate margin.
The same NFT is later re-deposited into the protocol.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Incomplete state cleanup during liquidation leaves residual lock flags.

operational_questions: Can the locker address be manually cleared by an admin?
Does this affect cross-margin deposits of the same NFT?
validation_requirements: Liquidate an isolate loan, re-deposit the NFT, and attempt to withdraw it.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an active attack, but a severe operational failure.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Liquidator receives NFT and sells it on the open market.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Unsuspecting user buys the NFT and deposits it into BendDAO V2.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User attempts to withdraw the NFT and transaction permanently reverts.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent loss of user assets (NFTs stuck in protocol).
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade to clear the locker addresses.

validation_result.impact_summary: After isolate liquidation, the NFT's lockerAddr is never cleared. If the NFT is re-deposited (or kept via supplyAsCollateral), it becomes permanently stuck because all withdrawal and mode-change operations require lockerAddr == address(0).
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The executeIsolateLiquidate function in IsolateLogic.sol deletes loanData and processes the NFT transfer via either erc721TransferIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate or erc721DecreaseIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate. Neither of these functions, nor executeIsolateLiquidate itself, resets tokenData.lockerAddr to address(0). The lockerAddr was originally set to address(this) during executeIsolateBorrow. In contrast, executeIsolateRepay correctly calls VaultLogic.erc721SetTokenLockerAddr(nftAssetData, params.nftTokenIds[vars.nidx], address(0)) on full repay. This is an omission in the liquidation path.

For the !supplyAsCollateral path: the NFT leaves the protocol but stale lockerAddr data persists in storage. If the same NFT is re-deposited (validateDepositERC721 only checks tokenData.owner == address(0), not lockerAddr), the withdrawal will permanently revert.

For the supplyAsCollateral path: the NFT stays in the protocol with the new owner (liquidator), but the lockerAddr is still the old address. The new owner cannot withdraw or change supply mode.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible by comparing executeIsolateRepay (which correctly clears lockerAddr) with executeIsolateLiquidate (which does not). Both erc721DecreaseIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate and erc721TransferIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate only modify owner and supplyMode fields, not lockerAddr. The validateWithdrawERC721 function strictly requires lockerAddr == address(0) for all withdrawals.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Clears owner and supplyMode but omits clearing lockerAddr. This leaves stale locker data that blocks future withdrawals of re-deposited NFTs.
Transfers ownership to the liquidator but doesn't clear lockerAddr. The liquidator receives an NFT that is permanently stuck because lockerAddr != address(0).
No call to erc721SetTokenLockerAddr(nftAssetData, tokenId, address(0)) anywhere in the liquidation flow. Compare with executeIsolateRepay which correctly calls it.

This strict check on lockerAddr == address(0) is the gate that prevents withdrawal. Since liquidation never clears lockerAddr, any re-deposited NFT (or NFT kept via supplyAsCollateral) will fail this check permanently.

calling_sequence: 3a. If !supplyAsCollateral: calls erc721DecreaseIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate (clears owner, supplyMode; NOT lockerAddr) then erc721TransferOutLiquidity (NFT leaves pool)
3b. If supplyAsCollateral: calls erc721TransferIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate (transfers owner; NOT clearing lockerAddr)
4. For path 3a: NFT is on open market. New buyer deposits NFT via BVault.depositERC721 -> validateDepositERC721 only checks owner == address(0), deposit succeeds
5. New owner calls BVault.withdrawERC721 -> validateWithdrawERC721 checks lockerAddr == address(0) -> REVERTS with ASSET_ALREADY_LOCKED_IN_USE
6. For path 3b: Liquidator immediately stuck, cannot withdraw or change supply mode due to lockerAddr check
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The root cause is in executeIsolateLiquidate which calls delete on loanData but never calls VaultLogic.erc721SetTokenLockerAddr(nftAssetData, tokenId, address(0)). This contrasts with executeIsolateRepay which correctly clears lockerAddr. The stale lockerAddr then blocks all withdrawal attempts because validateWithdrawERC721 (ValidateLogic.sol) and the isolate-specific check in executeWithdrawERC721 (SupplyLogic.sol) both require lockerAddr == address(0). Additionally, executeSetERC721SupplyMode also checks lockerAddr == address(0), so mode changes are also blocked.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration is relevant to this code-level defect.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an active attack but a severe operational failure. Scenario: (1) User A deposits BAYC #100 in isolate mode, borrows against it. (2) Loan becomes undercollateralized, goes through auction. (3) Liquidator wins auction, calls isolateLiquidate with supplyAsCollateral=false. (4) BAYC #100 is transferred to liquidator. lockerAddr remains set in protocol storage. (5) Liquidator sells BAYC #100 on OpenSea. (6) User B buys BAYC #100 and deposits it into BendDAO V2 (deposit succeeds as only owner==address(0) is checked). (7) User B attempts to withdraw BAYC #100 — transaction permanently reverts with ASSET_ALREADY_LOCKED_IN_USE. NFT is stuck. For supplyAsCollateral=true, the liquidator's NFT is immediately stuck after liquidation.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL: Any NFT that goes through isolate liquidation will have a permanently stale lockerAddr. If the NFT is ever re-deposited into the same pool, it becomes permanently stuck. For supplyAsCollateral=true, the liquidator's NFT is immediately stuck. This represents a permanent loss of user funds (NFTs) that can only be resolved through a contract upgrade. The issue occurs naturally during normal protocol operation without any attacker involvement.
recommended_mitigations: In executeIsolateLiquidate, add VaultLogic.erc721SetTokenLockerAddr(nftAssetData, params.nftTokenIds[vars.nidx], address(0)) before deleting the loan data, for each token in the loop.
In erc721DecreaseIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate, add tokenData.lockerAddr = address(0) alongside the owner and supplyMode clearing.
In erc721TransferIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate, add tokenData.lockerAddr = address(0) when transferring ownership.
Add a check in validateDepositERC721 to require tokenData.lockerAddr == address(0) as a defense-in-depth measure.
Consider adding an admin function to manually clear stale lockerAddr for already-affected NFTs as an emergency fix.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy protocol with test NFT and ERC20. 2. User A deposits NFT in isolate mode. 3. User A borrows against NFT. 4. Manipulate oracle to make loan undercollateralized. 5. Bidder places auction bid. 6. Advance time past auction duration. 7. Call isolateLiquidate with supplyAsCollateral=false. 8. Verify lockerAddr is still set in storage (not address(0)). 9. Transfer NFT to User B externally. 10. User B deposits same NFT into the pool (should succeed). 11. User B attempts to withdraw — should revert with ASSET_ALREADY_LOCKED_IN_USE. Also test supplyAsCollateral=true: after liquidation, new owner attempts withdrawal — should revert.
uncertainties: Cannot verify exact line numbers as the source lacks line annotations, but the pattern and code paths are confirmed from the provided source.
additional_testing_needed: Verify that no admin function exists (outside provided code) that can manually clear lockerAddr
Test both supplyAsCollateral=true and supplyAsCollateral=false paths
Test cross-mode deposit after liquidation (deposit as cross instead of isolate) to verify it's also affected
Check if there are any upgrade mechanisms that could be used as emergency fix
